title: Devilled ham sandwich
subtitle: Potted spiced ham spread on white bread with butter
excerpt: Devilled ham is cooked ham worked with mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce into a butter-enriched paste. Spread generously on white bread, it makes a sandwich that is sharp, savoury, and far better than plain ham. A traditional British tea-table filling.
sourceNotes: British potted and devilled preparations have existed since at least the 18th century. Devilling refers to seasoning with hot spices such as mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce. Devilled ham appeared in Victorian cookery books as both a table spread and a sandwich filling, and is associated with afternoon tea and supper.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The devilled ham keeps in the fridge for 3 days. Assemble the sandwiches just before serving.

content.text: Devilled ham transforms leftover or shop-bought cooked ham into a much more interesting sandwich filling. The heat from the cayenne and mustard cuts through the richness of the butter and ham, and the Worcestershire sauce adds depth. Use a food processor for a smooth paste, or finely chop the ham and mix by hand for a coarser texture. Both are correct.
Finely chop the ham or pulse it in a food processor until it forms a coarse paste. Transfer to a bowl.
Beat in the softened butter, mustard, cayenne, Worcestershire sauce, and black pepper. Taste and adjust: it should be savoury, spiced, and slightly hot. Chill for 30 minutes if time allows.
Butter the bread. Spread a generous layer of devilled ham on four slices. Top with the remaining slices, press, and cut into triangles or fingers to serve.

items.symptom: The filling is too dry and crumbly
items.cause: Not enough butter, or ham was very lean.
items.fix: Add another 10 to 20 g of softened butter and beat again.

items.cause: Cayenne and mustard not assertive enough.
items.fix: Devilled preparations should have noticeable heat. Increase the cayenne by a small pinch and taste again.

items.symptom: The spread is lumpy and hard to work with
items.fix: The butter must be fully softened before mixing. Cold butter will not incorporate evenly.

content.text: Devilling in British cooking refers to the use of hot condiments to transform leftover or simple ingredients into something more interesting. It appears in Victorian and Edwardian cookery books as a treatment for cooked meats, cold poultry, and kidneys. Devilled ham in particular was a standard tea-table item and cold supper dish well into the mid-20th century. It is one of those preparations that faded from fashion without ever becoming actually wrong.
